title: The New York Fed DSGE Model Forecast—June 2026
gist: The NY Fed's DSGE model revised 2026 core PCE inflation sharply upward from 2.4% to 3.1%—attributing the miss to tariff and energy (mark-up) shocks viewed as transient—while simultaneously upgrading the output outlook due to positive AI investment (MEI) shocks, and raising the short-run real natural rate r* by 10 basis points to 2.0% for 2026.

themes.theme: NY Fed DSGE: Stagflationary Miss Partially Offset by AI Investment Boom

insights.claim: The NY Fed DSGE model revised 2026 core PCE inflation from 2.4% to 3.1% (Q4/Q4), attributing the forecast error to 'mark-up shocks' that may capture the effects of both tariff and energy shocks—but the model treats these as transient, with inflation forecasts largely unchanged for 2027 onward.
insights.speaker: NY Fed — Liberty Street Economics
insights.so_what: The DSGE's tariff/energy inflation attribution with a 'transient' label is key: if the model is right, the current inflation spike does not justify sustained Fed tightening—a dovish read for rates despite the upward revision.
insights.quote: the model revised its inflation forecasts upward in 2026, from 2.4 to 3.1 percent. The DSGE attributes the forecast error to mark-up shocks, which may capture the effects of both tariff and energy shocks. The model views the impact of these shocks as vanishing by the end of the year
insights.locator: the SPF turns out to be correct

insights.claim: The DSGE model is more upbeat on output than in March, seeing positive 'marginal efficiency of investment' (MEI) shocks from the AI-related boom pushing output up especially in 2026—though adverse markup shocks are expected to push down growth in the future. GDP growth forecast: 1.2% (Q4/Q4) for 2026.
insights.speaker: NY Fed — Liberty Street Economics
insights.so_what: The DSGE explicitly models AI as a positive investment shock lifting near-term output; this provides formal quantitative validation for the view that AI capex is already boosting US growth in 2026, even as the model sees future headwinds from markup shocks.
insights.quote: The model does see the positive growth effect of the AI-related boom in investment—positive MEI (marginal efficiency of investment) shocks push output up, especially in 2026

insights.claim: The DSGE model raised its estimate of the short-run real natural rate of interest (r*) by 0.1 percentage point relative to March, to 2.0%, 1.7%, and 1.3% for 2026, 2027, and 2028 respectively, partly reflecting higher total factor productivity (TFP).
insights.speaker: NY Fed — Liberty Street Economics
insights.so_what: Rising r* estimates driven by higher TFP (partially AI-related) imply a structurally higher neutral rate—supporting the view that the Fed may need to keep rates higher for longer even after transient inflation fades; this is bearish for rate-sensitive assets.
insights.quote: The model's predictions for the short-run real natural rate of interest (r*) increased by 0.1 percentage point relative to March (2.0, 1.7, and 1.3 percent for 2026, 2027, and 2028, versus 1.9, 1.6, and 1.3 percent), partly reflecting higher total factor productivity (TFP).
insights.locator: Projections for the short-run real natural rate

source_meta.author: Marco Del Negro, Keshav Dogra, Elena Elbarmi, Donggyu Lee, Michael Pham
